content.assessment: Structural delay of tracking to age 14+ directly targets the mechanism most strongly implicated in inequality by this lens's evidence base (early tracking, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01), but the evidence is explicit that delay alone is insufficient without bundled curriculum, ability-grouping, and choice-regulation reform, and that within-school sorting would likely persist or intensify as a substitute channel.
opportunities: A unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase could reduce the geographic access disparity currently driven by distance to selective schools (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
Could reduce Roma segregation if catchment rules are genuinely enforced, since current segregation is concentrated in early sorting into weaker tracks (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
strengths: Addresses the core structural driver most strongly linked to inequality across comparable systems: early tracking age (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)
Explicitly proposes bundling with anti-sorting catchment rules, which the evidence indicates is necessary rather than optional for the delay mechanism to reduce (not merely relocate) segregation
Would reduce the SES-skewed access advantage currently concentrated in early academic tracks (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
threats: Within-school classroom sorting by SES is documented as a substitute mechanism that would likely absorb displaced sorting pressure even if formal tracking structure is abolished (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)

Constitutional free-choice protections could permit continued informal sorting via school choice even after formal tracking delay, echoed by international evidence that choice-driven segregation persists independent of tracking age

Elite capture of any transition process could preserve advantage for families able to navigate whatever gatekeeping replaces formal tracks, given documented pattern of capital-intensive competition for entry (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
weaknesses: Without curriculum and ability-grouping reform bundled in, delay alone may fail to reduce inequality, since track-level advantage could simply reassert itself later or through classroom-level sorting
Church/private schools sit outside state maintainer jurisdiction and could continue informal early selection, undermining uniform delay across the system (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
